system.title: Informatics Campaigns
system.subtitle: An escape-room learning environment for informatics competencies under the Czech RVP standards.

inventory.title: Inventory
inventory.hoverHint: Hover over an item to inspect it.
inventory.selectPrompt: Select an item to inspect its details.
inventory.readableHint: This recovered document can be opened again at any time.
inventory.unreadableHint: This item is stored equipment, not a readable document.
inventory.defaultSummary: Recovered equipment preserved in the campaign inventory.

module_ram.summary: A recovered RAM buffer module carrying short-term restoration data.
module_ram.body:
EJECTED MEMORY CARTRIDGE

The repaired mainframe pushed a maintenance cartridge through the airlock slot. The casing marks it as a RAM buffer module used to hold short bursts of restored data while damaged systems reboot.

You pocket it before the slot locks again.

module_loop.summary: A loop-control module used to repeat short command routines.
module_loop.body:
OVERRIDE DUMP // LOOP CONTROL MODULE

The override executable flushed a hardware shim onto the maintenance desktop. Its registry tag identifies it as a loop control module used to repeat short command routines inside patrol firmware.

It looks portable enough to carry deeper into the facility.

usb_decryptor.summary: A legacy decryptor still accepted by older archive systems.
usb_decryptor.body:
LEGACY PATCH CONTAINER // OPENED

Inside the old admin patch bundle you find a compact USB decryptor once used to open sealed archive drawers during emergency audits.

The label says the cipher routines are outdated, but still accepted by the transit hub.

storage.summary: Transit archive access restored. The recovered storage marker confirms the records can be opened again.

sector_map.summary: The restored digital twin core summarizing the evacuation sector model.
sector_map.body:
DIGITAL TWIN CORE

The recovered core contains the final stabilized model of the evacuation wing. Pressure, seal, thermal, scrubber, battery, and rail data now map cleanly onto the right subsystems.

It proves that the facility can only report a safe state once the correct relationships between data sources are restored.

flight_chip.summary: A service-drone flight routine preserved as a recovered autopilot chip.
flight_chip.body:
AUTOPILOT FLIGHT CHIP

The merged corridor repair routine has been committed into a portable flight chip. It stores the shortest repeatable service path that reaches every damaged panel before returning the drone to the exit route.

The chip is a reminder that good automation depends on a model, a route, and a procedure that can be executed reliably.

archive_notice.summary: Council notice describing the disputed charter and the need to restore archival order.
archive_notice.body:
COUNCIL NOTICE

Today's inspection found a conflict between the main charter and its copied registry books. Until the authentic version is proven, no official approval may be issued.

Anyone entering the archive must first restore the shelf order and recover the guides used to arrange the records.

shelf_guide.summary: Shelf-ordering guide explaining the archive's chronological sorting rule.
shelf_guide.body:
ARCHIVE SHELF GUIDE

The soaked board still preserves one rule: shelves were sorted by year of entry, and only inside the same year by record type.

If you want to find anything, you must restore the card order first. Otherwise every later clue will sit in the wrong place.

clerk_index.summary: Index procedure showing how names, streets, parcels, and districts verify one another.
clerk_index.body:
INDEX PROCEDURE

Each name in the residence book must point to the correct street, parcel, and district. If even one field is missing, the record has to be checked against another book.

Meaning appears only when the related fields verify one another.

ledger_bundle.summary: Rebuilt ledger rows preserving the matched municipal record combinations.
ledger_bundle.body:
RESTORED LEDGER BUNDLE

Record 1: Eliska -> Bridge Street -> Parcel A-12 -> North District
Record 2: Jan -> Square Street -> Parcel B-07 -> Market District
Record 3: Marta -> Mill Street -> Parcel C-19 -> River District

These rows are now preserved as verified combinations rather than loose scraps of data.

wax_seal.summary: Seal-handling rule confirming that authenticity must be checked before copying content forward.
wax_seal.body:
SEAL REVIEW NOTE

When records disagree, we must never rewrite the main charter from the first convenient note. First we verify the seal, then we compare the index, address, and parcel number.

Without a seal, even a well-written page is only a suspicious copy.

routing_stamp.summary: Routing reminder that document flow changes which evidence can be trusted or recovered.
routing_stamp.body:
ROUTING CONTROL STAMP

At high traffic, check the routing flow first. Otherwise the paper disappears into the wrong drawer and will never be found again.

A correct filing system is not only about storage. It is also about the rules that decide where information travels next.

witness_note.summary: Witness statement about where the authentic charter and its copy were last placed.
witness_note.body:
WITNESS NOTE

An old town messenger states that the authentic charter was returned to the inner vault after review, while one working copy remained in the clerk's drawer.

If that claim is true, the other records must reflect it as well.

district_map.summary: Transparent district overlay explaining the three map layers used in the archive comparison.
district_map.body:
DISTRICT OVERLAY MAP

A thin transparent sheet shows three map layers. One marks the north district, one the riverside parcels, and one the market quarter. Only the correct layer assignments will reveal where the disputed records truly belong.

charter_fragment.summary: Verified charter fragment preserved with its final archival verdict.
charter_fragment.body:
VERIFIED CHARTER FRAGMENT

Review outcome:
- Charter page -> Inner Vault -> Valid Wax Seal -> Authentic
- Registry copy -> Clerk Drawer -> Countermark -> Supporting
- Witness roll -> North Shelf -> Broken Seal -> Disputed

The fragment is now backed by matching archive location, seal state, and supporting evidence.

level1.title: Memory Defragmentation
level1.subtitle: Manual Bubble Sort Protocol
level1.story: The airlock mainframe is still alive, but barely. Its comparison bus is damaged, so it can only inspect neighboring memory blocks one pair at a time. Step through the defragmentation pass manually and keep the restored cache stable long enough to reopen the sector.
level1.objective: This puzzle simulates how a machine can sort data using repeated local comparisons. You are acting as the missing comparison logic: inspect the highlighted pair and decide whether the values stay in place or swap.
level1.hint: Compare the two highlighted memory blocks. If the LEFT value is GREATER than the RIGHT value, press SWAP. Otherwise, press KEEP.
level1.noActivePair: All remaining blocks are aligned. The current pass is complete.
level1.pairRelationGreater: {{left}} is greater than {{right}}. This pair must swap before the pass can continue.
level1.pairRelationOrdered: {{left}} is already less than or equal to {{right}}. This pair can stay in place.
level1.mistakeSwap: {{a}} < {{b}} — they are already in order. Press KEEP.
level1.mistakeKeep: {{a}} > {{b}} — they are out of order! Press SWAP.
level1.awaitingSort: MEMORY BLOCKS CORRUPTED. AWAITING MANUAL DEFRAGMENTATION...
level1.sortComplete: DEFRAGMENTATION COMPLETE. MEMORY ALIGNED. DOOR UNLOCKED.
level1.cartridgeReady: MAINFRAME CACHE RELEASED. RETURN TO THE AIRLOCK AND COLLECT THE EJECTED MEMORY CARTRIDGE.
level1.tokenGranted: ACCESS TOKEN GRANTED. PROCEEDING TO NEXT ZONE...

level2.title: PATROL ROUTE OVERRIDE
level2.subtitle: Loop Runner Protocol
level2.story: The restored airlock leads to a dead patrol corridor. Its security bot will only reopen the blast door if you feed it a short reusable movement routine instead of individual commands. Build the loop, set how many times it repeats, and test the route against the corridor map.
level2.objective: This puzzle models how a computer uses loops to repeat the same short procedure multiple times. Your goal is to write one compact command pattern that the robot can execute again and again until it reaches the exit.
level2.awaitingProgram: SECURITY BOT OFFLINE. PROGRAM A PATROL LOOP TO REACH THE EXIT...
level2.doorUnlocked: ROUTE COMPLETE. BLAST DOOR OVERRIDE ACCEPTED.
level2.hint: Build a repeating pattern of commands (loop body). The robot executes all commands in order, then repeats. Set the number of repetitions and press RUN. Watch the robot — if it hits a wall or misses the exit, adjust your commands or iteration count.
level2.emptyBody: Add commands to the loop body...
level2.hitWall: Robot crashed into a wall! Adjust your commands.
level2.missedExit: Robot didn't reach the exit. Try changing the iteration count or commands.
level2.success: Route complete! The robot reached the exit.

level3.title: SIGNAL RELAY
level3.subtitle: Emergency Beacon Reconstruction
level3.awaitingSignal: COMMS ARRAY POWERED. REBUILD THE RELAY PACKET BEFORE THE FACILITY SIGNAL WINDOW CLOSES.
level3.beaconLocked: PRIMARY CHANNELS MUST BE STABLE BEFORE THE FINAL BEACON GRID CAN ACCEPT INPUT.
level3.failedTransmission: TRANSMISSION REJECTED. THE RELAY CHECKSUM STILL CONFLICTS WITH THE RECOVERED PATTERN.
level3.signalRecovered: BEACON ACCEPTED. A REMOTE EMERGENCY NODE IS ANSWERING THE CALL.
level3.decryptorGranted: ARCHIVE TOOL RECEIVED: USB DECRYPTOR. SECURE FILES CAN NOW BE OPENED.
level3.archiveLinkReady: ARCHIVE TRANSIT HUB LOCATED. PROCEED TO THE RECOVERED LOGS.
level3.hint:
Three damaged channels describe the same emergency signal in machine form. Replay the recovered pulse on each panel, rebuild the lit cells, and then transmit the full packet. The first two channels power the final beacon grid.

This is about encoding information for a machine, not guessing a word.
level3.readyToTransmit: All three channels are aligned. The relay is ready to send.
level3.partialSignal: Keep restoring the panels. Matching patterns mean the machine can read the same information again.
level3.objective: The patrol override restored local movement control, but the outside network is still silent. Rebuild the emergency relay packet so the old response network can tell you where the survivors were redirected.
level3.story:
The communications bay was built for staff who had to send machine-readable distress bursts during system blackouts.

Your repaired RAM module can hold short packets. The loop controller can replay damaged pulse intervals. If the relay accepts your packet, the facility may finally answer with a route beyond the sealed sector.

status.buffer: Buffer channel edited. Compare the lit cells with the recovered pattern.
status.pulse: Pulse channel edited. Keep the timing strip stable.
status.beacon: Beacon checksum edited. Final packet field updated.

buffer.title: RAM BUFFER
buffer.description: A short burst copied from damaged memory sectors. Rebuild the bit strip exactly as the buffer replays it.

pulse.title: LOOP OSCILLATOR
pulse.description: The patrol loop module recovered the rhythm but not the stored values. Restore the timing strip cell by cell.

beacon.title: BEACON CHECKSUM
beacon.description: The final relay grid remains locked until the first two channels agree. Then recreate the outgoing checksum field.

archiveBridge.title: ARCHIVE LINK ESTABLISHED
archiveBridge.subtitle: Emergency Response Playback
archiveBridge.summary: The beacon did not summon rescue. It woke an old emergency routing assistant that still has fragments of the evacuation log. Each file reveals why the facility failed and why the next system you face is not a machine problem anymore, but a record problem.

response.title: Emergency Node
response.heading: External Response // Relay-17
response.body:
Signal origin verified. Internal facility beacon recognized.

Primary evacuation command failed during archive corruption cascade.
Manual restoration recommended: recover staff transit ledgers before attempting sector release.

Attached: supervisor fragment, transit map shell.

supervisor.title: Supervisor Fragment
supervisor.heading: Supervisor Fragment // Shift Lead M. Varga
supervisor.body:
If a trainee reaches this terminal, listen carefully. The core AI didn't only lock doors. It scrambled records. Names were separated from sectors, sectors from shuttles, times from clearances.

You can still out-think it because you can read context, not just tokens. Rebuild the transit ledger and you'll find where the evacuees were rerouted.

transit.title: Transit Schematic
transit.heading: Transit Hub Overlay // Archive Transit Hub
transit.body:
Three shuttle routes remain partially intact. The hub still knows that each route needs one person, one departure sector, one launch time, and one shuttle assignment.

One drawer is sealed, but your decryptor can open it. Recover the final missing record and the route map should illuminate a safe path deeper into the facility.

level4.title: ARCHIVE TRANSIT HUB
level4.subtitle: Evacuation Ledger Rebuild
level4.awaitingRebuild: THE LOGISTICS BOARD IS CORRUPTED. REBUILD THREE CONSISTENT TRANSIT RECORDS TO TRACE THE EVACUATION ROUTE.
level4.slotMismatch: That record fragment belongs to a different archive column.
level4.recordUpdated: Record slot updated. Keep the board internally consistent.
level4.drawerUnlocked: Sealed drawer decrypted. The missing personnel record is back in circulation.
level4.incomplete: Some route slots are still empty.
level4.failedAudit: Archive audit failed. The routes still contradict the surviving logs.
level4.archiveRestored: Transit ledger restored. A survivor route is lighting up beyond the hub.
level4.storageGranted: ARCHIVE STORAGE MODULE RECOVERED. ROUTE SNAPSHOTS PRESERVED.
level4.hint:
The board stores structured records. Each row needs one person, one sector, one time, and one shuttle. Use the audit clues and the decrypted drawer to place every fragment into a consistent ledger.

You can select a card, then place it into a matching column. Click a filled slot with no card selected to clear it.
level4.drawerSubtitle: One missing record survived under sealed storage.
level4.manualDrawerUnlocked: Manual decrypt succeeded. The sealed drawer is now open.
level4.statusHint: Rebuild the ledger until every route row is complete, then run the archive audit.
level4.selectedFragmentHint: Select a fragment from the archive bank, then place it into a matching ledger column.

rules.3: The decrypted drawer holds the person assigned to the final route.
rules.6: Atlas was not assigned to Sector D.

manualDecrypt.title: Manual Cipher Override
manualDecrypt.body: No decryptor was recovered. The archive still exposes one weak operator shortcut if you can reverse the label cipher by hand.
manualDecrypt.hint: Shift lead Varga rotated protected names three letters forward in the alphabet before filing them. Reverse the shift to reveal the passenger hidden in this drawer.
manualDecrypt.prompt: Recover the original passenger name from QHUD.
manualDecrypt.failure: That decode does not match the archive cipher.

branchJunction.title: The facility opens into two live corridors.
branchJunction.body: The restored ledger flashes two viable routes deeper into the complex. The left corridor still has an active relay backbone. The right corridor feeds a dormant logistics sorter. Pick the way you trust and commit to it.
branchJunction.prompt: Choose a corridor to continue the sequel.

left.title: Left Passage
left.detail: Follow the communications trunk and rebuild a packet route through the damaged relay mesh. This path focuses on networks and how data moves across connected nodes.

right.title: Right Passage
right.detail: Enter the logistics branch and configure a decision system that routes cargo crates to the correct exits. This path focuses on conditions, branching, and algorithmic decision-making.

branchLeftRelay.title: Relay Spine
branchLeftRelay.story: The left corridor leads into a mesh of surviving relay nodes. The archive route is still there, but the packet keeps choosing degraded branches. Study each relay dossier, then build the only route that satisfies the restored network constraints.
branchLeftRelay.awaiting: Trace a packet route that matches every relay clue before you transmit.
branchLeftRelay.progress: Route extended. Keep following stable relay links.
branchLeftRelay.backtracked: Last relay removed. Try a different branch.
branchLeftRelay.invalidStep: That jump is not allowed. Packets can only travel across linked, unused relays.
branchLeftRelay.ready: Exit uplink reached. Validate the route to transmit the packet.
branchLeftRelay.failure: The packet still hits an unstable branch. Re-check the relay route.
branchLeftRelay.success: Relay path accepted. Your clearance packet disappears deeper into the complex.
branchLeftRelay.boardHint: Hover a relay to inspect its dossier. Click a connected relay to extend the packet route or click the previous hop to backtrack.
branchLeftRelay.objective: This puzzle mirrors how data travels through a network graph. You need to combine node properties with connectivity constraints and send the packet through the only route that satisfies all conditions.

rules.1: Begin at the ingress port and end at the exit uplink.
rules.2: The first hop after ingress must use the cyan service band.
rules.3: The route cannot pass through any unstable relay.
rules.4: The middle relay must have the lowest latency among the stable relays reachable from the first hop, and the final prep relay must expose an even checksum gate.

branchLeftRelay.statusHint: Build the route visually, then transmit it once the exit uplink is connected.

branchRightDecision.title: Sorter Annex
branchRightDecision.story: The right passage opens into a cargo sorter that still tries to route emergency crates. Its switches lost their decision rules, so every crate is drifting toward the wrong chute. Rebuild the conditions at each junction and make the system classify incoming cargo correctly again.
branchRightDecision.awaiting: Set a rule for each junction so every crate reaches its intended bay.
branchRightDecision.updated: Switch condition updated. Watch how the crates reroute in the preview.
branchRightDecision.incomplete: At least one junction is still missing a decision rule.
branchRightDecision.failure: The sorter still misroutes part of the cargo stream.
branchRightDecision.success: Sorter logic restored. The annex accepts your routing profile and opens the deeper sector.
branchRightDecision.objective: This puzzle represents an algorithm with branching decisions. Each junction checks one property of the crate and sends it left or right. Choose the right condition so the whole decision tree works.

ruleGuide.1: The first junction should separate the healthy stream from the damaged one.
ruleGuide.2: The upper branch then needs to separate urgent crates from the rest.
ruleGuide.3: The lower branch finally separates amber-tagged cargo from cyan-tagged cargo.

branchRightDecision.statusHint: Choose three rules, verify the live simulation, then run the sorter.

junction_alpha.title: Junction Alpha
junction_alpha.body: Primary intake gate. It should split the cargo stream into stable and damaged traffic.

junction_beta.title: Junction Beta
junction_beta.body: Upper branch gate. It must separate urgent crates from routine deliveries.

junction_gamma.title: Junction Gamma
junction_gamma.body: Lower branch gate. It decides which damaged stream reaches each final repair bay.

rules.stable: If checksum is stable, send left.
rules.high_priority: If priority is high, send left.
rules.amber_tag: If crate tag is amber, send left.
rules.archive_tag: If archive tag is present, send left.
rules.low_priority: If priority is low, send left.
rules.cyan_tag: If crate tag is cyan, send left.

branchLeftBuffer.title: Packet Splice Bay
branchLeftBuffer.story: The relay spine carries your signal into a splice chamber with half a packet still scattered across emergency memory. Your RAM buffer module can stabilize five slots at once, but one fragment is just degraded echo noise. Rebuild the final frame in the only order the relay will accept.
branchLeftBuffer.awaiting: Load the restored fragments into the RAM buffer in the correct order.
branchLeftBuffer.updated: Fragment buffered. Keep assembling the relay frame.
branchLeftBuffer.removed: Fragment removed from the buffer. Re-check the sequence.
branchLeftBuffer.ready: All five RAM slots are loaded. Validate the splice order.
branchLeftBuffer.incomplete: The relay frame is still missing one or more fragments.
branchLeftBuffer.failure: That fragment order still breaks the outgoing packet.
branchLeftBuffer.success: Frame accepted. The relay backbone compresses into a single stable stream.
branchLeftBuffer.itemBlocked: The splice bay needs the RAM Buffer Module before it can hold a full relay frame.
branchLeftBuffer.objective: This puzzle mirrors how computers store and order packet fragments inside memory before transmission. Use the RAM buffer to keep only the correct fragments and assemble them in the sequence that satisfies all constraints.

rules.1: The relay frame begins with the boot handshake and ends with the checksum seal.
rules.2: The authentication fragment must sit immediately before the checksum seal.
rules.3: The parity fragment has to sit between the route fragment and the authentication fragment.
rules.4: The RAM module opens exactly five stable slots, and all five final slots must be used.
rules.5: The echo fragment is degraded noise and must stay outside the final transmission buffer.

branchLeftBuffer.statusHint: Stage five fragments in the buffer and transmit only when every slot is filled.

fragmentClues.boot: This frame initializes the relay packet. Nothing else can lead the transmission.
fragmentClues.route: The routing header must appear before the parity strip so downstream relays know which branch to verify.
fragmentClues.parity: The parity strip stabilizes the gap between routing and authentication.
fragmentClues.auth: Authentication cannot close the packet by itself; the checksum seal must come immediately after it.
fragmentClues.checksum: This fragment always ends the transmission. It seals every previous field into one verified frame.
fragmentClues.echo: This duplicate fragment is just degraded residue from the damaged relay. It should never enter the final buffer.

branchRightDecryptor.title: Manifest Cipher Locker
branchRightDecryptor.story: Past the sorter annex, a sealed archive locker still stores emergency manifests in an old shifted alphabet. Your USB decryptor can tune a different backward shift for each file. Find the correct mode for every header so the records become readable again.
branchRightDecryptor.awaiting: Set a decryptor mode for each manifest until every header resolves into a valid archive tag.
branchRightDecryptor.updated: Decryptor mode updated. Watch the preview field for a readable header.
branchRightDecryptor.incomplete: At least one manifest still has no decryptor mode selected.
branchRightDecryptor.failure: One or more headers are still scrambled. Re-tune the decryptor modes.
branchRightDecryptor.success: Cipher lock cleared. The decrypted manifests release the final archive latch.
branchRightDecryptor.itemBlocked: The manifest locker requires the USB Decryptor before any legacy header can be read.
branchRightDecryptor.objective: This puzzle focuses on information encoding and transformation. The same word can be represented in different forms, and a machine needs the right decoding rule to recover the original message.

rules.1: Each manifest uses one backward shift of 1, 2, or 3 letters.
rules.2: A correct shift produces a readable archive tag in the preview field.
rules.3: Every file must have a decryptor mode before you can release the locker.

branchRightDecryptor.statusHint: Tune each manifest until the preview resolves into a meaningful archive tag, then release the locker.

details.route: The route table records where evacuation traffic was redirected once the blast doors sealed.
details.crew: The crew log tracks who was still inside the sector when the emergency sorter switched to fallback rules.
details.maint: The maintenance file contains the hardware fixes staff used when corridor systems failed one after another.
details.quarantine: The quarantine note marks which sectors had to be isolated after the first corrupted cargo stream spread through the annex.

branchConvergence.title: The separate corridors bend back into the same spine.
branchConvergence.body: The relay branch and the sorting annex both spill back into the same maintenance artery. The central sector is running on emergency guesses now: the facility still sees the world, but it no longer understands what its own readings mean.
branchConvergence.detail: The network taught you to track moving information. The sorter showed how rules reshape outcomes. The final sector needs both at once: rebuild the model of the wing, then launch an automated repair routine that can stabilize the last corridor.

level7.title: SECTOR DIGITAL TWIN
level7.subtitle: Rebuilding The Evacuation Wing Model
level7.awaiting: The digital twin is still receiving data, but it keeps translating it into the wrong system states. Reconnect the raw inputs to the correct subsystems and set the right operating modes so the model finally reports a safe sector.
level7.invalidLink: That connection does not make sense. Sensors should feed subsystems, and subsystems should feed the final state nodes.
level7.linked: Data line rerouted. Watch which parts of the model start stabilizing.
level7.unlinked: Connection removed. The model is recalculating the sector state.
level7.modeUpdated: Subsystem mode updated. Check whether the new interpretation of the data produces a safe result.
level7.incomplete: The digital twin is still incomplete. Some sensor links or subsystem modes are missing.
level7.failure: The model still predicts an unsafe sector. Some relationships between the data and the system are wrong.
level7.success: The digital twin is reading the sector correctly again. The central model is switching from emergency estimates to verified state.
level7.unlock: The digital twin core has been restored. The precise sector model can now guide the final automated repair.
level7.objective: This puzzle shows the difference between data and information. Sensors provide raw values. Only when you connect those values to the correct parts of the system and choose the right interpretation mode do they become a model that can tell whether the sector is truly safe.
level7.hint: Containment uses pressure + seal integrity. Life support uses thermal + scrubber data. Transit uses battery + rail status. SAFE STATE needs containment and life support. EVAC READY needs transit.
level7.statusHint: Click a line to disconnect it. The goal is not to connect everything everywhere, but to turn raw data into one correct view of the sector.

sensor_pressure.title: Pressure Grid
sensor_pressure.body: Raw corridor pressure. Without context, it is only a number.

sensor_seal.title: Seal Monitor
sensor_seal.body: Reports how well the transition bulkheads are still holding.

sensor_thermal.title: Thermal Array
sensor_thermal.body: Heat readings from the occupied wing. Alone, they do not say whether the air is livable.

sensor_scrubber.title: Scrubber Filter
sensor_scrubber.body: Filter loop quality. It only becomes meaningful inside life support.

sensor_battery.title: Battery Bus
sensor_battery.body: Power reserve for movement systems and route scheduling.

sensor_rail.title: Evac Rail
sensor_rail.body: Status of the transport line for capsules and maintenance movement.

hub_containment.title: Containment Core
hub_containment.body: Decides whether the wing is physically holding together and keeping pressure inside safe corridors.

hub_life_support.title: Life Support Matrix
hub_life_support.body: Turns raw readings into information about whether the environment is actually survivable.

hub_transit.title: Transit Scheduler
hub_transit.body: Determines whether automated movement and evacuation routines can be deployed safely.

status_safe_state.title: SAFE STATE
status_safe_state.body: Final model output confirming the wing is sealed and habitable.

status_evac_ready.title: EVAC READY
status_evac_ready.body: Allows automated repair and movement routines to go live.

level8.title: MAINTENANCE DRONE AUTOPILOT
level8.subtitle: Debugging The Repair Loop
level8.awaiting: The central model is finally accurate, but the last corridor is still sealed. Build a short routine for the maintenance drone so it repairs panels when needed, turns only when blocked, and reaches the exit gate.
level8.programUpdated: Autopilot routine updated. Test whether the drone behaves correctly across the whole loop.
level8.incomplete: The program is still missing commands. Every loop slot must be filled.
level8.executing: The drone is entering the corridor. Watch the run and find where the routine breaks.
level8.wall: The drone hit a sealed bulkhead. The routine reacts incorrectly to a blocked path.
level8.panels: The drone moved forward, but at least one repair panel is still offline. Shift the repair step to the right point in the loop.
level8.failure: The autopilot still does not fully stabilize the corridor.
level8.success: The autopilot is clean. The drone repaired both panels and reached the final gate.
level8.unlock: The autopilot flight chip has been verified. The merged corridor is stable and the campaign can close.
level8.objective: This puzzle shows that an algorithm does not need to be long to be smart. You are building a tiny loop with a condition: the drone should repeatedly decide when to repair, when to turn, and when to simply move forward. The key is testing and debugging, not guessing.
level8.hint: The drone repeats the same short routine. It needs three behaviors: repair a panel, react to a blocked path, and otherwise keep moving. If the loop is correct, the same pattern carries it through both turns and both repairs.
level8.statusHint: Click a command in the deck and then a slot. Clicking a slot without a selected command clears it.

campaignDebrief.title: The key trail is readable again.
campaignDebrief.body: This was not only a checklist of tasks. You restored order inside data, procedures, and relationships between parts of a system. Each future campaign can use a different setting, but the goal stays the same: understand the problem, verify information, and make it reliable again.

left.title: Left Relay Branch
left.body: Inside the weakened network spine, you had to read node properties and find the single path that satisfied every constraint. That branch reinforced that information never travels randomly: it always obeys the structure of the network carrying it.

right.title: Right Decision Sorter
right.body: Inside the logistics wing, you built rules that split cargo into different outputs. That route showed how one change in a condition can flip the outcome of an algorithm.

campaignDebrief.systemsBody: Each restored module represents a tool or idea you used during the campaign. It is not only inventory. It is a map of how you moved from simple step-by-step control toward understanding and stabilizing a whole system.

1.title: Data is not the same as information
1.body: Raw readings solve nothing on their own. Meaning appears only when data is attached to the right model or rule.

2.title: A short algorithm can do serious work
2.body: Sequence, repetition, and simple conditions are enough to make a machine handle a more complex task. What matters is predicting, testing, and fixing mistakes.

3.title: Systems depend on relationships
3.body: The network, archive, model, and drone only work when you understand how their parts affect one another. Informatics is not just clicking controls. It is finding those links.

campaignDebrief.nextDirectiveBody: You can return to the menu, replay the campaign through another path, or continue into the next world once it is ready.

elem_7.title: The town archive speaks truth again.
elem_7.body: You did not repair a machine. You restored trust in a public record. You rebuilt the shelf order, reconnected torn ledgers, repaired the clerk workflow, set the request routing logic, and proved that map, index, and charter describe the same town.
elem_7.systemsBody: Each recovered item represents a verified part of the evidence chain. Together they form the record trail that keeps the town from approving the wrong version of its own history.
elem_7.nextDirectiveBody: The archive is readable again. You can return to the menu, replay the campaign, or continue into the next world once it is ready.

fast_track.title: Direct Archive Trail
fast_track.body: You rebuilt the ledger cleanly enough that the archive could send you straight into the procedural repair track. Your run emphasized confident data-linking: once the records aligned, the office could trust the next steps without an extra detour.

review_track.title: Secondary Review Trail
review_track.body: Your ledger reconstruction needed an additional review stop before the office could continue. That route underlined an important informatics lesson: when linked data becomes uncertain, a system needs a verification branch rather than blind confidence.

verified.title: Verified Verdict
verified.body: The final evidence board aligned without forcing a later correction. You reached a clean, well-supported conclusion and returned the charter with a strong audit trail behind it.

contested.title: Contested But Resolved
contested.body: You still reached the authentic charter, but only after the evidence board had to be corrected. The town receives the right verdict together with proof that weak reconstructions must be challenged and repaired.

1.title: Data without context is not enough
1.body: One card or one page does not prove the truth. Meaning appears when multiple records agree and can verify each other.

2.title: Procedure is part of informatics
2.body: Office workflow is not random. If one step is skipped or misplaced, errors appear just as easily as in a broken algorithm.

3.title: A model must match its records
3.body: The map, the index, and the charter are different views of the same reality. Informatics helps keep those models consistent.

errorBoundary.title: Critical Runtime Fault
errorBoundary.body: The current protocol encountered an unrecoverable UI error. Return to the main menu to restart the run safely.

gameContainer.puzzleNotFound: Puzzle logic not found for level {{level}}
gameContainer.exitConfirmBody: Returning to the main menu will end the current run for {{campaign}}.
gameContainer.exitConfirmNote: Closing an individual puzzle preserves your current run. Aborting the protocol does not.
gameContainer.gameOverBody: Security breach detected. All logical sectors have been frozen.

debug.title: Development Jump Menu
debug.body: Skip directly to any registered screen in the current campaign.
debug.autoSeed: Auto-seed prerequisite items and room progress

crt.desc: Enable retro scanlines and chromatic aberration

audio.desc: Master volume control for environment and FX ({{volume}}%)

commandSurface.title: Campaign Overview
commandSurface.body: Choose a world, tune the interface, or resume a suspended run.

desktopSim.title: Desktop Recovery Console
desktopSim.instructions: Open folders, inspect archived files, and execute the legacy override to restore the missing loop control module.
desktopSim.operatorNotesBody: Double-click any node to inspect it. Executables run immediately. Collected rewards are added to the run inventory the first time they are opened.
desktopSim.recoveryWorkspaceBody: Archived logs, admin tools, and dropped modules appear on this desktop surface.

pickups.usb_decryptor: USB Decryptor recovered from legacy admin tools.
pickups.module_loop: Loop Control Module recovered from the override dump.
pickups.module_loop_ready: Loop Control Module dropped to desktop. Recover it to proceed.

lore.decryptor:
LEGACY PATCH CONTAINER // OPENED

Inside the old admin patch bundle you find a compact USB decryptor once used to open sealed archive drawers during emergency audits.

The label says the cipher routines are outdated, but still accepted by the transit hub.
lore.loopModule:
OVERRIDE DUMP // LOOP CONTROL MODULE

The override executable flushed a hardware shim onto the maintenance desktop. Its registry tag identifies it as a loop control module used to repeat short command routines inside patrol firmware.

It looks portable enough to carry deeper into the facility.
lore.sys:
SYSTEM CONFIGURATION [RESTRICTED]

Memory allocation module bypassed.
Next zone requires a patrol bot to navigate a corridor grid.
The bot can only MOVE FORWARD, TURN LEFT, or TURN RIGHT.
Find a repeating pattern and use a loop.
lore.warn:
INCIDENT REPORT #04

The security bot has been deactivated. To reopen the blast door, someone must program a new patrol route.

The corridors form a zigzag pattern — the bot needs to navigate around walls.
HINT: Look for a short sequence of moves that repeats to cover the whole path.
lore.readme:
WELCOME TO THE STAFF PORTAL.

Reminder: The blast door override requires a correctly looping patrol route. A loop is a sequence of commands that repeats multiple times. Think about which commands move the bot through one section of the corridor, then how many times that pattern must repeat.

room.interactHint: Recovered objects now project field markers. Notes appear as tagged evidence cards, while active machinery opens a transit route deeper into the facility.
room.mysteriousNote:
Where am I? The last thing I remember... the facility AI went rogue and scrambled all the memory blocks. The mainframe's data bus is damaged — it can only compare two ADJACENT blocks at a time.

If you're reading this, we need to manually sort the memory to restore the system. The shift supervisor's log should explain the defragmentation procedure.
room.employeeLog:
SHIFT SUPERVISOR LOG

Emergency Defragmentation Protocol:
1. Look at two neighboring memory blocks
2. If the left one has a BIGGER value than the right one, SWAP them
3. If they are already in order, leave them and move on
4. Keep going until everything is sorted

This is called 'Bubble Sort' — the biggest values slowly bubble to the end, like air bubbles rising in water.
room.memoryCartridge:
The repaired mainframe has pushed a maintenance cartridge out through the airlock access slot. The casing marks it as a RAM buffer module, probably used to hold short bursts of restored data while damaged systems reboot.

You pocket it before the slot locks again.

notepad.title: Personal Logs
notepad.placeholder: Record protocol observations and sequences here...

archiveEntry.subtitle: Municipal Archive
archiveEntry.title: Archive Entrance Hall
archiveEntry.body: Floodwater reached the first hall and collapsed the opening shelves into a single pile. The town council still demands a rapid verdict on a disputed charter page, but first you need to understand how the records were originally arranged.
archiveEntry.objective: Read every clue. Once you understand the notice, the shelf guide, and the damage log, you can continue to the first shelf audit.
archiveEntry.instruction: Each clue explains a different part of the problem. Continue only after you understand why one surviving page is not enough on its own.

mayorNotice.title: Town Council Notice
mayorNotice.body:
COUNCIL NOTICE

Today's inspection found a conflict between the main charter and its copied registry books. Until the authentic version is proven, no official approval may be issued.

Anyone entering the archive must first restore the shelf order and recover the guides used to arrange the records.

shelfGuide.title: Archive Shelf Guide
shelfGuide.body:
The soaked board still preserves one rule: shelves were sorted by year of entry, and only inside the same year by record type.

If you want to find anything, you must restore the card order first. Otherwise every later clue will sit in the wrong place.

waterLog.title: Damage Report
waterLog.body:
The building steward reports that the north wall suffered the worst damage and that the auxiliary copies were scattered into nearby rooms.

The main charter may have survived, but its supporting evidence is now split across multiple sources.

clerkDesk.subtitle: Clerk Office
clerkDesk.title: Municipal Clerk Desk
clerkDesk.body: Open registers, sealing wax, and index drawers still lie across the desk. It is clear that the clerk was comparing several sources at once in order to trace the origin of one disputed page.
clerkDesk.objective: Read the diary, the index manual, and the margin note. Without them it will not be clear how the town records were meant to fit together.
clerkDesk.instruction: This room explains why shelf order alone is not enough. The real problem is that the same people and parcels appear in several record systems at once.

clerkDiary.title: Clerk Diary
clerkDiary.body:
DAILY ENTRY

When records disagree, we must never rewrite the main charter from the first convenient note. First we verify the seal, then we compare the index, address, and parcel number.

Without a seal, even a well-written page is only a suspicious copy.

indexManual.title: Index Manual
indexManual.body:
INDEX PROCEDURE

Each name in the residence book must point to the correct street, parcel, and district. If even one field is missing, the record has to be checked against another book.

Meaning appears only when the related fields verify one another.

marginNote.title: Margin Note
marginNote.body:
A short note in the margin reads: 'At high traffic, check the routing flow first. Otherwise the paper disappears into the wrong drawer and will never be found again.'

This is almost certainly a reference to the routing board in the next room.

ledgerFastTrack.subtitle: Record Corridor
ledgerFastTrack.title: Direct Review Slip
ledgerFastTrack.body: The rebuilt ledger rows agree so cleanly that the archive allows the file to proceed without a secondary stop. The clerk leaves only a short procedural briefing before handing the case to the workflow desk.
ledgerFastTrack.objective: Review both notes. This branch exists because the data links were restored cleanly enough to skip manual re-sorting.
ledgerFastTrack.instruction: A good information system does not only reject errors. It also rewards a clean reconstruction by shortening the path to the next verified step.

directReviewSlip.title: Direct Review Slip
directReviewSlip.body:
DIRECT REVIEW AUTHORIZED

The rebuilt ledger rows match across every field. No extra sorting clerk is required. The case may proceed directly to procedure repair.

routingBrief.title: Routing Brief
routingBrief.body: When linked records agree, the office saves time by following the shorter verified route. This is still a decision process: a system can branch because the data is reliable enough to skip an intermediate correction step.

ledgerReview.subtitle: Record Corridor
ledgerReview.title: Secondary Review Desk
ledgerReview.body: The archive accepts your repaired ledger, but only after routing it through a short secondary review. The case can continue, yet the office leaves a warning about what happens when linked data is repaired late.
ledgerReview.objective: Review both notes. This branch exists because the restored data needed a verification stop before the clerk trusted it enough to continue.
ledgerReview.instruction: Information systems need fallback branches when a result arrives with uncertainty. Extra verification is not failure. It is how a trustworthy process protects itself.

secondaryReviewNotice.title: Secondary Review Notice
secondaryReviewNotice.body:
SECONDARY REVIEW REQUIRED

The municipal ledger has been restored, but the chain of linked fields was not stable on the first pass. Route the file through the review desk before returning it to the main procedure.

procedureWarning.title: Procedure Warning
procedureWarning.body: When linked data becomes uncertain, the office must not pretend that nothing happened. The correct response is to branch into an additional verification step and only then continue with the main workflow.

evidenceVault.subtitle: Evidence Vault
evidenceVault.title: Room Of Stored Proof
evidenceVault.body: Beyond the last door lie maps, seal boxes, and witness notes. At this point the task is no longer only to repair books, but to prove that every model of the town points to the same reality.
evidenceVault.objective: Review all three clues. Only then will the overlay map and final evidence board make sense together.
evidenceVault.instruction: The map, the witness note, and the seal box are three different sources. In the finale you must show that they agree and support the same conclusion.

witnessNote.title: Witness Note
witnessNote.body:
An old town messenger states that the authentic charter was returned to the inner vault after review, while one working copy remained in the clerk's drawer.

If that claim is true, the other records must reflect it as well.

districtOverlay.title: District Overlay Map
districtOverlay.body: A thin transparent sheet shows three map layers. One marks the north district, one the riverside parcels, and one the market quarter. Only the correct layer assignments will reveal where the disputed records truly belong.

sealBox.title: Seal Box
sealBox.body: Inside the box are three marks: an intact wax seal, a broken seal, and an office countermark. Each belongs to a different kind of document, and none of them may be confused.

level1.subtitle: Shelf Audit
level1.title: Restore The Archive Cards
level1.story: Only part of the shelf guide survived. You need to return the cards to the correct chronological order before any connected records can be trusted or located in context.

feedback.start: Move the cards until they run from the oldest year to the newest.
feedback.updated: The order is changing. Check whether the timeline on the shelf now makes historical sense.
feedback.failure: The archive row is still not sorted from the oldest year to the newest.
feedback.success: The shelf order is restored. A clerk index has appeared in the next drawer.

level1.hint: The archivist sorted these cards only by year. The smallest number must be at the top and the largest at the bottom.

level2.subtitle: Cross-Checking Ledgers
level2.title: Reconnect The Municipal Ledger
level2.story: Several torn books preserve only fragments of names, streets, parcels, and districts. Rebuild three complete rows so the town ledger becomes trustworthy again.

feedback.start: Fill all three rows so that no field contradicts the others in the same row.
feedback.incomplete: At least one ledger row is still incomplete. Finish all four fields before validating.
feedback.updated: The rows are filling in. Keep checking whether the name, street, parcel, and district really belong together.
feedback.fieldMismatch: The selected value belongs to a different ledger column.
feedback.failure: Some field combinations still conflict. Re-check the full rows.
feedback.success: The municipal ledger is readable again and produced a bundle of confirmed records.
feedback.fastTrack: The ledger aligned cleanly. The archive grants a direct review slip and opens the shorter branch.
feedback.reviewTrack: The ledger is restored, but the archive sends it through a secondary review branch before continuing.

residentStreet.title: Residence Book
residentStreet.body: Use this table as the first join: each resident points to exactly one street.

streetParcel.title: Parcel Ledger
streetParcel.body: The second join maps each street to a parcel identifier.

parcelDistrict.title: District Register
parcelDistrict.body: The last join maps each parcel to the district where it belongs.

level2.hint: Treat this as linked data reconstruction. Join resident to street, street to parcel, and parcel to district. A row is only valid when all three joins stay consistent.

level3.subtitle: Office Workflow
level3.title: Restore The Clerk Procedure
level3.story: Without the correct workflow the office would approve the wrong copy. Rebuild the procedure in the proper order from request intake to secure duplicate storage.

feedback.start: Move the steps until they form a sensible office workflow from request intake to stored duplicate.
feedback.updated: The workflow is taking shape. Make sure verification happens before annotation and storage.
feedback.failure: The sequence still does not reflect the correct clerk procedure.
feedback.success: The office procedure is repaired and the wax seal confirms the next clue.

level3.hint: The request has to arrive first, then the seal is checked, then the index is consulted, then the margin note is written, and only then is the duplicate stored.

level4.subtitle: Index Routing
level4.title: Set The Decision Rules
level4.story: Requests for record access pass through three routing nodes. Each node needs the correct rule so the document continues into the proper archive branch.

feedback.start: Assign one rule to each node. Only the right combination keeps requests in the correct archive branches.
feedback.updated: The routing board is changing. Compare each rule with the description of its node.
feedback.failure: Some routing rules still send requests into the wrong branch.
feedback.success: The index board is repaired and a routing stamp confirms the next stage.

junction_alpha.label: Node A
junction_alpha.title: First Split
junction_alpha.body: Decides whether a request continues through the verified main route or falls into secondary review.

junction_beta.label: Node B
junction_beta.title: Main Archive Branch
junction_beta.body: Allows only documents carrying the archive mark to continue through the confirmed route.

junction_gamma.label: Node C
junction_gamma.title: Secondary Review
junction_gamma.body: Catches the papers marked in blue and redirects them into a special control branch.

level4.hint: The first node separates verified records from the rest. The second accepts the archive mark, and the third selects the blue-marked papers.

level5.subtitle: Overlay Map
level5.title: Align The Map Layers
level5.story: Each data bundle belongs to a different municipal map layer. If the assignments are wrong, the town model will contradict the restored ledgers and witness notes.

feedback.start: Click to cycle the layer numbers until every bundle belongs to the correct overlay.
feedback.updated: The overlays are changing. Compare them with what you already know about the north, river, and market districts.
feedback.failure: The map layers still do not match the restored records.
feedback.success: The map now agrees with the evidence and the district overlay is ready for the finale.

north_register.title: North Register
north_register.body: Contains house references from the upper edge of the town.

river_parcels.title: River Parcels
river_parcels.body: Tracks plots beside the canal and mill.

market_rolls.title: Market Rolls
market_rolls.body: Belongs to the area around the square and marketplace.

council_minutes.title: Council Minutes
council_minutes.body: Contains references to the north district and confirms council actions there.

level5.hint: Two bundles may point to the same layer. What matters is that the model stays consistent with the restored records.

level6.subtitle: Final Reconciliation
level6.title: Verify The Authentic Charter
level6.story: The last task is the hardest: build a final evidence set from documents, storage locations, seals, and conclusions so the town can identify the authentic charter version.

feedback.start: Assemble three evidence rows so each document matches the correct storage place, seal, and conclusion.
feedback.incomplete: At least one evidence row is still incomplete. Fill every document, storage, seal, and verdict slot before validating.
feedback.updated: The evidence board is taking shape. Check full rows, not single fields in isolation.
feedback.fieldMismatch: The selected card belongs to a different evidence column.
feedback.failure: The final comparison still does not support the correct charter reconstruction.
feedback.success: The authentic charter has been identified and the verified fragment can return to the town.
feedback.verified: The evidence network aligned cleanly. The town receives a fully verified verdict.
feedback.contested: The evidence board required correction before it stabilized. The verdict is authentic, but the archive records it as a contested reconstruction resolved through review.

documentArchive.title: Provenance Ledger
documentArchive.body: This source records where each document was last stored.

documentSeal.title: Seal Register
documentSeal.body: This source links each document type to the seal found with it.

sealVerdict.title: Verdict Rubric
sealVerdict.body: This rule table classifies what each seal state means for the final verdict.

level6.hint: Treat this as a classification pipeline. First map document to storage, then document to seal, and finally seal to verdict. The whole row must survive all three checks.

elem_6.title: Facility Recovery Protocol
elem_6.desc: Restore scrambled systems, recover staff records, and reach the next sealed sector.

elem_7.title: Archive of the Missing Town
elem_7.desc: A historical mystery campaign about broken ledgers, missing pages, and rebuilding a trustworthy town record system.
